sources.description: Title: Hell's House Summary: Jimmy idolizes bootlegger Matt, and when he refuses to implicate his friend, he is sent to reform school. He befriends Shorty, a boy with a heart condition, and escapes to let the world know about the brutal conditions. Directed by: Howard Higgin Actors: Production Company: B.F. Zeidman Productions Ltd. Release Date: 30 January 1932 (USA) Aspect Ratio: 1.37 : 1 Jimmy Mason idolizes bootlegger Matt Kelly who is dating Peggy. Unwilling to squeal on his idol, Jimmy winds up in a cruel reformatory where a new- found friend is dying from abuse. Jimmy escapes to report the abuse. Matt and Peggy help him once he has escaped.

sources.title: Hell's House (1932) [Drama]
sources.description:
"Hell's House" is a 1932 American drama film directed by Howard Higgin. The screenplay by Paul Gangelin and B. Harrison Orkow, set during the waning days of the Prohibition era, is based on a story by Higgin.

When orphaned Jimmy Mason is taken in by his Aunt Emma and Uncle Henry, he meets their boarder Matt Kelly, who impresses the young man with his boastful swagger and alleged political connections, although in reality he's a bootlegger. The boy's life is disrupted when, as one of Kelly's hired hands, he refuses to identify his boss during a police raid and is sentenced to three years of hard labor in reform school, where he befriends a sickly boy named Shorty, who eventually is sent to solitary confinement.

When Jimmy realizes his new pal is seriously ill and desperately needs medical attention, he escapes and goes to Kelly and Kelly's girl friend, Peggy Gardner, for help. Peggy contacts newspaper columnist Frank Gebhardt, who is anxious to expose the conditions at the state industrial school. The authorities find Jimmy at Gebhardt's office, but before they can apprehend him Kelly admits his involvement in the bootlegging operation and the boy is set free. He discovers Shorty has died, victimized by a corrupt system.

---

Directed by Howard Higgin, produced by B. F. Zeidman, written by Paul Gangelin and B. Harrison Orkow, starring Bette Davis as Peggy Gardner, Pat O'Brien as Matt Kelly, Junior Durkin as Jimmy Mason, Frank Coghlan Jr. as Shorty, Emma Dunn as Emma Clark, Charley Grapewin as Henry Clark, Morgan Wallace as Frank Gebhardt, Hooper Atchley as Captain Of The Guard, Wallis Clark as Judge Robinson and James A. Marcus as Superintendent Charles Thompson. 

---

Source: "Hell's House" Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. Wikimedia Foundation, Inc.. 1 March 2013. Web. 20 April 2013. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hell%27s_House.

sources.title: Hell's House (1932) BETTE DAVIS
sources.description:
Stars: Junior Durkin, Bette Davis, Pat O'Brien
Writer/Director: Howard Higgin

In this Pre-Code Hollywood melodrama, when an orphaned teenager lands in a brutal reform school after refusing to squeal on his bootlegger pal, he later escapes to let the world know about its cruel conditions.

sources.title: 1932 - Hell's House - Casa correccional - Howard Higgin - VOSE
Hell’s House 1932 | Colorized Public Domain Crime Drama with Bette Davis and Pat O’Brien
sources.description:
Hell’s House 1932 is an American pre-Code crime drama film directed by Howard Higgin and produced by B. F. Zeidman. This restored and colorized public domain feature highlights the struggles of juvenile delinquency, the corruption of reform schools, and the social injustices faced by troubled youth during the Depression era. Featuring an early performance by Bette Davis alongside Pat O’Brien, this film captures both the raw social commentary of its time and the star power of actors who would later become icons of Golden Age Hollywood. By reviving this classic in vivid color, modern audiences in the United States, Europe, and worldwide can rediscover its urgency and historical significance.

Plot Summary:
The story follows Jimmy Mason (played by Junior Durkin), a naïve and innocent boy whose life takes a tragic turn after he befriends bootlegger Matt Kelly (Pat O’Brien). Drawn into Kelly’s dangerous world, Jimmy is arrested for his involvement in illegal activities, despite his limited understanding of the consequences. To protect Kelly, Jimmy takes the fall and is sent to a brutal reform school, where neglect, cruelty, and systemic corruption dominate daily life.

Inside the institution, Jimmy befriends a fellow inmate who is seriously ill. Despite his efforts to seek medical attention, the reform school staff’s indifference leads to tragedy. Meanwhile, Kelly, realizing the consequences of his actions, struggles with his own guilt. Bette Davis plays Peggy, Kelly’s girlfriend, who sees Jimmy’s suffering and becomes a voice of conscience, pushing for justice and redemption. The film builds toward an emotional climax exposing the failures of the system and the cost of misplaced loyalty.

Cast and Crew:
• Director: Howard Higgin
• Producer: B. F. Zeidman
• Story by: Paul Gangelin and Howard Higgin
• Cinematography: Ira H. Morgan
Starring:
• Junior Durkin as Jimmy Mason
• Bette Davis as Peggy Gardner
• Pat O’Brien as Matt Kelly
• Frank Coghlan Jr. as Shorty
• Hooper Atchley as Thompson
• Emma Dunn as Mrs. Clark
• Sheila Terry as Grace

Film Significance:
Hell’s House stands as both a gripping drama and a piece of social critique. It reflects America’s growing concerns over youth crime, the failures of juvenile justice, and the exploitation of vulnerable children during the Depression era. Bette Davis, then at the beginning of her career, delivers a strong performance that foreshadowed her future as one of Hollywood’s most powerful leading ladies. Pat O’Brien, already making a name for himself in crime dramas, embodies the flawed bootlegger torn between greed and morality. The film remains a historically important artifact of pre-Code cinema, where Hollywood was freer to tackle controversial issues before the strict enforcement of the Hays Code.

Enhanced Public Domain Explanation:
This film is in the public domain, which means it is completely free of copyright restrictions and can be legally shared, viewed, studied, and preserved without limitation. Colorized Public Domain restores and enhances these historic works through careful digital cleaning, visual restoration, and colorization techniques. Our mission is to ensure that timeless classics like Hell’s House remain available to a worldwide audience. By presenting them in color and modern quality, we make them more engaging and accessible to younger generations who might otherwise overlook black-and-white cinema. This process respects the original film while providing a new layer of appreciation for today’s viewers.

Why Watch This Colorized Edition:
The original 1932 release was presented in black and white, common for the pre-Code era. While historically authentic, its stark presentation can feel dated for modern viewers. By offering a restored and colorized version, we highlight the emotional depth of the performances and immerse audiences in a more vibrant cinematic experience. This colorized edition bridges the gap between history and accessibility, making it easier for today’s viewers to engage with the film’s story and message while still respecting its original artistry.
